description: Mesoamerican glyph systems — structure, conventions, and generative notes for each culture's writing tradition.

glyph_systems.type: Full writing system — logographic + syllabic (CV syllabary)

visual_structure.block_shape: Roughly square cartouche; rounded corners; slight border channel around each block
visual_structure.reading_order: Left to right, top to bottom, in paired vertical columns (L-R across the pair, then down)
visual_structure.internal_subdivision: Main sign in center; affixes at top-left (superfix), bottom-right (subfix), or infixed inside main sign
visual_structure.size_variation: Large main sign with small affixes; or full-body head variant filling entire block
visual_structure.line_weight: Consistent medium weight; no thick-thin calligraphic variation (unlike Chinese brush)

sign_types.description: A single glyph represents an entire word; e.g., the AJAW (lord) glyph = face with cruller + dotted cheek
Each sign represents a consonant-vowel combination; ~100 signs in the syllabary; stacked to spell words phonetically
Numbers 1–13 and some day names can be written as deity head profiles; stylized deity head fills the glyph block
sign_types.character: Highly individual and calligraphic; each head has specific diagnostic marks (cruller under eye, flower, dots, infixes)

sign_types.description: Especially elaborate inscriptions show full body figures interacting, with entire bodies as glyphs; rare luxury feature
examples: Full-figure number series at Quirigua, Copan

number_system.example: 17 = 3 bars + 2 dots stacked; 20 = 1 dot in next position with shell below

glyph_systems.calendrical_glyphs: See calendars.json for Tzolk'in day signs, Haab' months, Long Count periods
glyph_systems.generative_hint: Each glyph block is a contained unit — roughly square, with a thick outer border channel. Internal design uses complex interlocking curves. For a 'Maya text wall': arrange in a 2-column grid, left column then right column, reading L-R then repeat. Allow about 40% of the internal area for the main sign, 20% for affixes. Rounded cartouche corners are important — they soften the otherwise angular grid.

glyph_systems.type: Pictographic with limited phonetic rebus elements; not a full writing system
glyph_systems.decipherment_status: Largely interpretable; phonetic system partially reconstructed

visual_structure.block_shape: Variable — name-glyphs tend to be roughly square; place-glyphs are hillform; event-glyphs are pictographic
visual_structure.reading_order: No strict linear order for most texts; following a visual narrative; codex books read along a red line path
visual_structure.labeling_convention: Figures are identified by labels attached near the head; place of action is labeled with place-glyph near scene

sign_types.description: Each person has a pictographic name composed of day-sign + personal marker; phonetic rebus encoding common
examples: Motecuhzoma = anger (motequi) + noble (zoma) — a frowning face + noble dress

sign_types.description: Mountain/hill (tepetl) form as base + attribute on top; e.g., Tenochtitlan = cactus (nochtli) + rock (tetl) on a hill
sign_types.shape: Inverted trapezoid (hill form) with identifying element at top or on face

sign_types.description: The 20 day signs of the Tonalpohualli (see calendars.json); pictographic
sign_types.usage: Personal birth-date names; calendar dates; integrated into codex narrative

sign_types.description: Specific pictographs encode recurring events: footprints = journey, fire drilling = new year, severed head = conquest, speech scroll = speech/naming, shield+spear = war
Dots (1), flags (20), feathers (400), incense bags (8000); different from Maya bar-dot system
sign_types.colors: Flags/banners in yellow; feathers in green; bags in turquoise; dots in white or red

codex_types.name: Codex Borgia
codex_types.style: Rich colors; symbolic density; western Nahua origin

codex_types.name: Codex Mendoza
codex_types.type: Historical + tribute record; commissioned after Conquest; bilingual

codex_types.name: Codex Borbonicus
codex_types.type: Ritual calendar; Aztec origin; two sections: tonalpohualli + xiuhpohualli

codex_types.name: Tonalamatl Aubin
codex_types.type: Sacred almanac; trecena patrons and auguries

glyph_systems.generative_hint: For Aztec glyph generation: start with a strong silhouette (recognizable outline = the meaning). Fill with flat color. Add phonetic attribute as a small attached element. Black outline must be bold and decisive. Place-glyphs: hill silhouette as base, attribute on top. Day-signs: 20 distinctive compact pictographs (see aztec.md for list).

glyph_systems.type: Early logographic/syllabic; one of the oldest scripts in the Americas
glyph_systems.decipherment_status: Partially deciphered; ongoing; ~3–4 centuries older than Maya script evidence

visual_structure.context: Primarily carved into stone slabs and monument sides; not organized in tight grid columns
visual_structure.block_shape: Variable; some signs are oval cartouche, some are more free-form
visual_structure.reading_direction: Not fully established; possibly top-to-bottom or context-dependent

sign_types.description: Upside-down or scroll-identified head below a figure = captive's name or origin; hill glyph with suffix = place name
Zapotec 260-day calendar equivalents; distinct forms from Aztec; 52-year calendar shared
Combinations identifying political figures; visible in Monte Albán III period

glyph_systems.generative_hint: Zapotec glyphs appear as carved bas-relief elements alongside figural compositions, not in a separate text zone. For generation: attach simple geometric cartouche elements near figures as labels; use hillform base for place references. Style is simpler/blockier than Maya — fewer internal subdivisions.

glyph_systems.type: Pictographic narrative; phonetic rebus elements; strip-format codex reading
glyph_systems.decipherment_status: Substantially interpreted; place-glyph system well documented

visual_structure.format: Accordion-folded deer-skin strip; both sides painted; follows a winding red-line path (boustrophedon)
visual_structure.reading_path: A bold red line (or band) indicates the sequence; figures and events occur within this path frame
visual_structure.figure_style: Articulated, stiff figures with precise costume details; never obscured or partially hidden

sign_types.description: Hill/mountain base (ñuu) + distinctive attribute: stripe color, element on top, mouth element
260-day calendar day signs used as birth-name components; e.g., 8-Deer = 8 dots + deer head
Year bundles = bound reed bundle with year-sign flag; 52-year cycle; embedded in narrative along the red path
Marriage = rope connecting two figures; war = spear and shield; death = body in burial bundle; speech = scroll

glyph_systems.generative_hint: Mixtec text is not a text-wall — it is a flowing picture strip. For generation: place figures along a winding path; attach day-sign name labels near each figure's head; show event symbols between figures (rope for marriage, spear for war). The red path line is always visible as a structural element. Landscape = minimal; place-glyphs as compact labels.

glyph_systems.culture: Epi-Olmec / Isthmian (Gulf Coast, Isthmus of Tehuantepec)
glyph_systems.type: Syllabic + logographic; possibly the earliest true writing in the Americas
glyph_systems.decipherment_status: Partially deciphered (one key text); related to proto-Zoquean language

visual_structure.block_shape: Rectangular cartouches in vertical columns; some integration with figural imagery
visual_structure.style: Blocky, rigid; some resemblance to early Maya forms

glyph_systems.generative_hint: Treat Epi-Olmec as a transitional style between Olmec visual mass and Maya glyph grids. Use rectangular block columns with heavy borders, simple internal geometric patterns. Useful for conveying 'ancient' Gulf Coast aesthetic.
